news_summary: Today's technology news highlights major advancements in ultra-fast, energy-efficient wireless communication, breakthrough insights in fusion physics, innovative 3D molecule synthesis, and cutting-edge computer chip developments poised to support the growing demands of AI.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine des communications sans fil ultra-rapides et économes en énergie, des découvertes révolutionnaires en physique de la fusion, des méthodes innovantes de synthèse moléculaire en 3D, ainsi que des développements de pointe en matière de puces informatiques, destinés à répondre aux besoins croissants de l'intelligence artificielle.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan importantes avances en comunicaciones inalámbricas ultrarrápidas y energéticamente eficientes, descubrimientos revolucionarios en física de la fusión, síntesis molecular 3D innovadora y desarrollos de vanguardia en chips informáticos, preparados para satisfacer las crecientes exigencias de la inteligencia artificial.

articles.title: Laser-powered wireless hits 360 Gbps and uses half the energy of Wi-Fi
articles.summary: A new breakthrough in wireless technology could dramatically boost internet speeds while cutting energy use—by switching from radio waves to light. Researchers have developed a tiny chip packed with dozens of miniature lasers that can transmit massive amounts of data simultaneously, reaching speeds over 360 gigabits per second in early tests.
articles.content:
Fast, reliable wireless connections are essential in everyday life. Video calls, streaming, virtual reality, and connected devices all rely on networks that are already under heavy strain. Today, most wireless communication depends on radio-based technologies like Wi-Fi and cellular networks. While these systems have enabled global connectivity, they are running into growing challenges such as crowded radio frequencies, signal interference in busy indoor environments, and increasing energy demands as more devices come online.

One emerging solution is optical wireless communication, which uses light instead of radio waves to transmit data. Light offers significantly more available bandwidth, avoids interference with existing wireless systems, and can be directed with high precision. These advantages make it especially appealing for indoor spaces like offices, homes, hospitals, data centers, and public venues where many users need fast connections at the same time.

In a study published in Advanced Photonics Nexus, researchers developed a compact optical wireless transmitter that delivers both extremely high speeds and improved energy efficiency. The system is built around a tiny chip containing an array of semiconductor lasers, combined with an optical design that carefully controls how light is distributed. Together, these components create a scalable platform for high-capacity indoor wireless communication.

Tiny Laser Array Sends Massive Data

At the core of the system is a custom-designed 5 × 5 array of vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers, known as VCSELs. These infrared lasers are commonly used in data centers and sensing technologies because they are efficient and capable of operating at very high speeds. They can also be manufactured in large arrays using standard semiconductor fabrication methods.

Each laser in the array can be controlled independently and transmit its own stream of data. By running multiple lasers at the same time, the system dramatically increases total data capacity compared to a single light source. The entire array fits on a chip smaller than a millimeter, making it suitable for compact wireless access points and potentially small enough to integrate into devices such as smartphones.

The researchers produced the chip using established semiconductor techniques and mounted it on a custom circuit board. Early testing showed consistent performance across the array, with stable output and support for high-speed data transmission.

Record-Breaking Optical Wireless Speeds

To test the system, the team created a free-space optical link spanning two meters. Each laser transmitted data using a modulation method that splits information into multiple closely spaced frequency channels. This approach maximizes bandwidth efficiency and adapts to changes in signal quality.

Out of the 25 lasers, 21 were active during testing. Individual lasers reached data rates between roughly 13 and 19 gigabits per second. Combined, the system achieved a total data rate of 362.7 gigabits per second. This is among the highest reported speeds for a chip-scale optical wireless transmitter paired with a free-space receiver.

The researchers noted that performance was limited by the bandwidth of the commercial photodetector used in the experiment. With more advanced receivers, the same system could potentially reach even higher speeds.

Shaping Light for Multiuser Connections

Using many light beams at once introduces a key challenge: preventing overlap that can cause interference. To solve this, the researchers designed an optical system that precisely shapes and directs each beam.

A microlens array first aligns and straightens the light from each laser. Additional lenses then organize the beams into a structured grid of square illumination areas at the receiving surface. This layout ensures that each beam covers a specific region with minimal overlap.

Tests showed that the light distribution achieved more than 90 percent uniformity across the illuminated area at a distance of two meters. This structured approach allows different beams to be assigned to different users or devices within the same room.

The team also demonstrated multiuser capability by activating several lasers at once. In a test with four simultaneous beams, each connection remained stable, delivering a combined data rate of about 22 gigabits per second. The results confirm that multiple optical links can operate at the same time without significant interference.

Lower Energy Use Than Wi-Fi

Improving energy efficiency is critical as wireless data demand continues to rise. Traditional radio-based systems require more power to support higher speeds, increasing both costs and environmental impact.

The optical wireless system uses laser sources that are inherently energy efficient and capable of high-speed operation without complex power demands. As a result, it consumes much less energy per bit of transmitted data compared to conventional Wi-Fi systems. Measurements showed an energy use of about 1.4 nanojoules per bit, roughly half that of leading Wi-Fi technologies under similar conditions.

Complementing Existing Networks

Researchers emphasize that optical wireless technology is not meant to replace Wi-Fi or cellular networks. Instead, it can work alongside them, handling high-capacity data traffic in indoor environments and reducing congestion on radio-based systems.

Looking ahead, similar systems could be built into ceilings, lighting fixtures, or wireless access points, delivering fast, secure, and energy-efficient connections to many users simultaneously. By combining compact laser arrays, high-speed transmission, and precise optical control, this approach offers a practical path toward next-generation indoor wireless networks that deliver greater performance without increasing energy consumption.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant breakthrough in wireless communication technology that offers dramatically higher data speeds and improved energy efficiency. This innovation has broad implications for indoor connectivity, reducing network congestion and energy consumption, benefiting many users and environments. The detailed explanation of the technology and its potential applications demonstrates substantial positive real-world impact and significance.
articles.personality_title: New laser chip boosts indoor wireless speeds while using half the energy of Wi-Fi
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most wireless connections today use radio waves, like Wi-Fi and cellular signals. These systems are getting crowded and use more energy as more devices connect, causing slower speeds and interference, especially indoors.

**What happened** – Scientists created a tiny chip with 25 small lasers that send data using light instead of radio waves. In tests, this chip reached speeds over 360 gigabits per second, much faster than current Wi-Fi, while using about half the energy. The lasers are arranged in a small 5 by 5 grid and can each send their own data stream.

**Impact** – This technology uses light beams that can be aimed precisely, reducing interference and allowing many users to connect at once with strong signals. It works well indoors in places like offices or homes, where many devices need fast internet. Using light also saves energy, which helps reduce costs and environmental effects compared to regular Wi-Fi.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to improve the system with better receivers to reach even higher speeds. They also want to build this technology into everyday devices or ceiling lights to provide fast, energy-saving wireless connections alongside existing Wi-Fi and cellular networks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new laser-based wireless chip offers much faster and more energy-efficient indoor internet by sending data with light instead of radio waves.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle puce laser améliore la vitesse du sans-fil intérieur tout en consommant deux fois moins d'énergie que le Wi-Fi
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aujourd'hui, la plupart des connexions sans fil utilisent des ondes radio, comme le Wi-Fi ou les réseaux cellulaires. Ces systèmes sont de plus en plus saturés et consomment plus d'énergie à mesure que le nombre d'appareils connectés augmente, ce qui ralentit la vitesse et crée des interférences, surtout à l'intérieur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé une toute petite puce avec 25 petits lasers qui transmettent des données en utilisant la lumière au lieu des ondes radio. Lors des tests, cette puce a atteint des vitesses de plus de 360 gigabits par seconde, bien plus rapide que le Wi-Fi actuel, tout en utilisant environ deux fois moins d'énergie. Les lasers sont disposés en grille 5 par 5 et peuvent chacun envoyer leur propre flux de données.

**Impact** – Cette technologie utilise des faisceaux lumineux dirigés avec précision, ce qui réduit les interférences et permet à plusieurs utilisateurs de se connecter en même temps avec des signaux solides. Elle fonctionne bien à l'intérieur, dans des lieux comme les bureaux ou les maisons où beaucoup d'appareils ont besoin d'internet rapide. Utiliser la lumière permet aussi d'économiser de l'énergie, ce qui réduit les coûts et l'impact sur l'environnement comparé au Wi-Fi classique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer le système avec des récepteurs plus performants pour atteindre des vitesses encore plus élevées. Ils envisagent aussi d’intégrer cette technologie dans des appareils du quotidien ou dans des luminaires pour offrir des connexions rapides et économes en énergie, en complément des réseaux Wi-Fi et cellulaires existants.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle puce sans fil à base de laser offre un internet intérieur beaucoup plus rapide et économe en énergie en transmettant les données avec la lumière au lieu des ondes radio.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo chip láser mejora la velocidad del wifi interior usando la mitad de energía que el Wi-Fi
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hoy en día, la mayoría de las conexiones inalámbricas usan ondas de radio, como el Wi-Fi y las redes celulares. Estos sistemas están cada vez más saturados y consumen más energía a medida que se conectan más dispositivos, lo que provoca velocidades más lentas e interferencias, especialmente en interiores.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos crearon un chip muy pequeño con 25 láseres que transmiten datos usando luz en lugar de ondas de radio. En pruebas, este chip alcanzó velocidades superiores a 360 gigabits por segundo, mucho más rápido que el Wi-Fi actual, y usó alrededor de la mitad de energía. Los láseres están organizados en una cuadrícula de 5 por 5 y cada uno puede enviar su propia señal de datos.

**Impacto** – Esta tecnología usa haces de luz que se pueden dirigir con precisión, reduciendo interferencias y permitiendo que muchos usuarios se conecten al mismo tiempo con señales fuertes. Funciona bien en interiores, como oficinas o casas, donde muchos dispositivos necesitan internet rápido. Usar luz también ahorra energía, lo que ayuda a reducir costos y efectos ambientales en comparación con el Wi-Fi común.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar el sistema con mejores receptores para lograr velocidades aún más altas. También quieren integrar esta tecnología en dispositivos cotidianos o luces de techo para ofrecer conexiones inalámbricas rápidas y que ahorran energía junto con las redes Wi-Fi y celulares existentes.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo chip inalámbrico basado en láser ofrece internet interior mucho más rápido y eficiente en energía al enviar datos con luz en lugar de ondas de radio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, glowing chip embedded with a precise 5×5 grid of tiny radiant laser beams spreading outward in distinct, colorful shafts of light, each beam illuminating separate, softly glowing squares on a smooth surface two meters away, symbolizing high-speed, energy-efficient wireless data transmission indoors.

articles.title: Physicists just solved a strange fusion mystery that stumped experts
articles.summary: Fusion scientists have solved a long-standing mystery inside tokamaks, the donut-shaped machines designed to harness fusion energy. For years, experiments showed that escaping plasma particles hit one side of the exhaust system far more than the other, but simulations couldn’t explain why. Now, researchers have discovered that the rotation of the plasma itself plays a crucial role—working together with sideways particle drift to create the imbalance.
articles.content:
Scientists have struggled for years to explain a curious pattern inside tokamaks, the doughnut-shaped machines designed to one day produce electricity by fusing atoms. Inside these devices, superheated plasma is held in place by magnetic fields. Some of those particles eventually escape from the core and travel toward the exhaust system, called the divertor.

When the particles reach the divertor, they hit metal plates, cool, and rebound. (The returning atoms help fuel the fusion reaction.) However, experiments have consistently revealed an unexpected imbalance. Far more particles strike the inner divertor target than the outer one.

This uneven distribution is more than just a curiosity. It has major implications for future fusion reactors. Engineers must know exactly where particles will land in order to design divertors that can withstand extreme heat and stress. Until now, the leading explanation focused on cross-field drifts, which describe how particles move sideways across magnetic field lines within the divertor. But simulations that included only this effect failed to reproduce what experiments were showing, raising doubts about whether models could reliably guide reactor design.

Plasma Rotation Emerges as the Missing Factor

New research has uncovered a key piece of the puzzle. Scientists found that toroidal rotation, the motion of plasma as it circles around the tokamak, strongly influences where particles ultimately end up in the exhaust system.

Using the SOLPS-ITER modeling code, researchers simulated particle behavior under a range of conditions. Their results, published in Physical Review Letters, showed that simulations only matched real-world measurements when plasma rotation was included alongside cross-field drifts. This alignment between models and experiments is essential for designing fusion systems that can operate reliably outside the lab.

"There are two components to flow in a plasma," said Eric Emdee, an associate research physicist at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory (PPPL) and lead author of the study. "There's cross-field flow, where particles drift sideways across the magnetic field lines, and parallel flow, where they travel along those lines. A lot of people said cross-field flow was what created the asymmetry. What this paper shows is that parallel flow, driven by the rotating core, matters just as much."

Simulations Match Reality at Last

To test their idea, the team modeled plasma behavior in the DIII-D tokamak in California. They ran four different scenarios, toggling cross-field drifts and plasma rotation on and off. The results were clear. None of the simulations matched experimental data until one critical ingredient was added: the measured core rotation speed of 88.4 kilometers per second.

Once both effects were included, the models closely reproduced the uneven particle distribution seen in real experiments. The combined influence of sideways drift and rotation proved much stronger than either factor on its own.

Designing Fusion Systems for Real Conditions

The findings highlight an important connection between the rotating plasma core and the behavior of particles at the edge of the system. Accurately capturing this relationship will be essential for predicting how exhaust particles move in future reactors.

Better predictions mean better engineering. With a clearer understanding of where heat and particles will concentrate, designers can build divertors that are more resilient and better suited to real operating conditions.

In addition to Emdee, the research team included Laszlo Horvath, Alessandro Bortolon, George Wilkie and Shaun Haskey of PPPL; Raúl Gerrú Migueláñez of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology; and Florian Laggner of North Carolina State University.

This work was supported by the DOE's Office of Fusion Energy Sciences, using the DIII-D National Fusion Facility, a DOE Office of Science user facility, under awards DE-AC02-09CH11466, DE-FC02-04ER54698, DE-SC0024523, DE-SC0014264 and DE-SC0019130.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in fusion energy research, solving a long-standing mystery about plasma behavior in tokamaks. This discovery improves the accuracy of simulations critical for designing more resilient fusion reactors, which has broad implications for future clean energy production and benefits society at large.
articles.personality_title: Scientists solve key mystery about plasma behavior in fusion reactors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tokamaks are donut-shaped machines designed to create energy by fusing atoms. Inside them, hot plasma is held by magnetic fields, but some particles escape and hit parts called divertors. For years, scientists noticed more particles hitting one side of the divertor than the other, but their computer models couldn’t explain why.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that the plasma’s rotation around the tokamak plays a big role in this uneven particle hitting. Using advanced computer simulations, they found that the plasma’s spinning combined with sideways particle movement causes more particles to strike one side. This matched real experiments for the first time.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it helps scientists understand exactly where particles go inside fusion reactors. Knowing this allows engineers to design parts that can handle heat and damage better, making future fusion reactors safer and more efficient.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this new understanding to improve reactor designs and simulations. This will help build stronger divertors and bring fusion energy closer to practical use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding plasma rotation to models solves a long-standing puzzle about particle behavior, improving fusion reactor design for the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques résolvent un mystère clé sur le comportement du plasma dans les réacteurs à fusion
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tokamaks sont des machines en forme de beignet conçues pour produire de l’énergie en fusionnant des atomes. À l’intérieur, un plasma chaud est maintenu par des champs magnétiques, mais certaines particules s’échappent et frappent des parties appelées divertors. Pendant des années, les scientifiques ont remarqué que plus de particules frappaient un côté du divertor que l’autre, mais leurs modèles informatiques ne pouvaient pas expliquer pourquoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que la rotation du plasma autour du tokamak joue un grand rôle dans cette répartition inégale des particules. Grâce à des simulations avancées, ils ont montré que la rotation du plasma combinée au déplacement latéral des particules provoque ce déséquilibre, ce qui correspond pour la première fois aux expériences réelles.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre exactement où vont les particules dans les réacteurs à fusion. Cela permet aux ingénieurs de concevoir des pièces capables de mieux résister à la chaleur et aux dommages, rendant les futurs réacteurs à fusion plus sûrs et plus efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette nouvelle compréhension pour améliorer la conception des réacteurs et les simulations. Cela aidera à construire des divertors plus solides et à rapprocher l’énergie de fusion d’une utilisation pratique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ajout de la rotation du plasma aux modèles résout un mystère ancien sur le comportement des particules, améliorant la conception des réacteurs à fusion pour l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos resuelven un misterio clave sobre el comportamiento del plasma en reactores de fusión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los tokamaks son máquinas en forma de dona diseñadas para crear energía fusionando átomos. Dentro de ellos, el plasma caliente se mantiene con campos magnéticos, pero algunas partículas escapan y golpean partes llamadas divertores. Durante años, los científicos notaron que más partículas golpeaban un lado del divertor que el otro, pero sus modelos informáticos no podían explicar por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores descubrieron que la rotación del plasma alrededor del tokamak juega un papel importante en esta distribución desigual de partículas. Usando simulaciones avanzadas, encontraron que el giro del plasma combinado con el movimiento lateral de las partículas causa que más partículas golpeen un lado, coincidiendo por primera vez con los experimentos reales.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a entender exactamente a dónde van las partículas dentro de los reactores de fusión. Esto permite a los ingenieros diseñar piezas que puedan soportar mejor el calor y el daño, haciendo que los futuros reactores de fusión sean más seguros y eficientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este nuevo conocimiento para mejorar el diseño y las simulaciones de los reactores. Esto ayudará a construir divertores más fuertes y acercará la energía de fusión a un uso práctico.

**Resumen en una frase** – Incluir la rotación del plasma en los modelos resuelve un misterio antiguo sobre el comportamiento de las partículas, mejorando el diseño de reactores de fusión para el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, doughnut-shaped tokamak viewed from above, with swirling vibrant plasma streams inside rotating gracefully around its core; inside the ring, two contrasting metal plates symbolizing the inner and outer divertor targets, one bathed in brighter, denser particle flows and the other dimmer, connected by flowing lines representing magnetic fields and particle drifts, all rendered in soft, natural hues of orange, blue, and gray to evoke energy and harmony in fusion science.

articles.title: Mix-and-match synthesis of 3D small molecules
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00809-0Chemistry that forms an omnipresent type of carbon–carbon bond enables the modular synthesis of 3D organic molecules — a boon for drug discovery and materials design.
articles.content:
Small organic molecules underpin modern life, from medicines and flavours to advanced materials. Much of this functional diversity comes from shape: modest changes in a molecule’s 3D structure can completely change its properties. To explore these structural effects efficiently, chemists often synthesize libraries of compounds through the Lego-like assembly of molecules from interchangeable chemical building blocks1. However, such approaches are only as useful as the types of carbon–carbon (C–C) bond that form between the building blocks during assembly. Writing in Nature, Zhang et al.2 report chemistry that not only enables the modular and iterative synthesis of a ubiquitous type of C–C bond — C(sp3)–C(sp3) bonds, which connect carbons that are already attached to three other atoms — but that also controls the 3D configuration of the attached atoms.

Nature 652, 303-304 (2026)

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-00809-0

References Lehmann, J. W., Blair, D. J. & Burke, M. D. Nature Rev. Chem. 2, 0115 (2018). Zhang, X., Palka, K. T., Zhang, M. & Morken, J. P. Nature 652, 359–364 (2026). Leitereg, T. J., Guadagni, D. G., Harris, J., Mon, T. R. & Teranishi, R. Nature 230, 455–456 (1971). Cherney, A. H., Kadunce, N. T. & Reisman, S. E. Chem. Rev. 115, 9587–9652 (2015). Choi, J. & Fu, G. C. Science 356, eaaf7230 (2017). Noguchi, H., Hojo, K. & Suginome, M. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 129, 758–759 (2007). Blair, D. J. et al. Nature 604, 92–97 (2022). Download references

Competing Interests D.J.B is listed as an inventor on patents relating to (tetramethyl N-methyliminodiacetic acid) (TIDA) boronates used in modular synthesis. Financial support to D.J.B. was provided by the National Institutes of Health R35GM159997.

Related Articles

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in chemistry enabling modular synthesis of 3D small molecules with controlled 3D configuration, which has broad implications for drug discovery and materials design, benefiting society at large. The story is focused and provides sufficient detail about the scientific advance and its potential impact.
articles.personality_title: New chemistry method builds 3D molecules with precise shapes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Small organic molecules are important in many areas like medicine, flavors, and materials. The way these molecules are shaped in three dimensions (3D) can change how they work. Scientists create many different molecules by connecting small chemical parts, but the connections they can make have limits.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Zhang and colleagues discovered a new chemistry technique that allows them to build 3D molecules by connecting carbon atoms in a special way. These carbon atoms are already attached to three other atoms, making the bond type called C(sp3)–C(sp3). This method not only links the parts together but also controls the 3D shape of the molecule precisely.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it gives scientists a new tool to create libraries of 3D molecules with exact shapes. Having control over shape is key in designing better medicines and materials. Before, it was harder to make these specific types of carbon connections with shape control, so this method opens new possibilities.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this new method to build a variety of molecules to test in drug discovery and materials research. It may speed up the process of finding new medicines and innovative materials with useful properties.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new chemistry method now lets scientists build 3D molecules with precise shapes by connecting carbon atoms in a controlled way, aiding drug and material design.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode chimique pour construire des molécules 3D avec des formes précises
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les petites molécules organiques sont importantes dans de nombreux domaines comme la médecine, les arômes et les matériaux. La forme en trois dimensions (3D) de ces molécules peut changer leur fonctionnement. Les scientifiques créent de nombreuses molécules différentes en reliant de petites parties chimiques, mais les connexions possibles étaient limitées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Zhang et ses collègues a découvert une nouvelle technique chimique qui permet de construire des molécules 3D en reliant des atomes de carbone d’une manière spéciale. Ces atomes de carbone sont déjà attachés à trois autres atomes, ce qui forme un type de liaison appelé C(sp3)–C(sp3). Cette méthode relie les parties tout en contrôlant précisément la forme 3D de la molécule.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle offre aux scientifiques un nouvel outil pour créer des collections de molécules 3D avec des formes exactes. Contrôler la forme est clé pour concevoir de meilleurs médicaments et matériaux. Avant, il était plus difficile de faire ces liaisons spécifiques avec contrôle de la forme, donc cette méthode ouvre de nouvelles possibilités.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette nouvelle méthode pour construire diverses molécules à tester en recherche de médicaments et de matériaux. Cela pourrait accélérer la découverte de nouveaux médicaments et matériaux innovants avec des propriétés utiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode chimique permet désormais aux scientifiques de construire des molécules 3D avec des formes précises en reliant des atomes de carbone de manière contrôlée, facilitant la conception de médicaments et matériaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método químico para construir moléculas 3D con formas precisas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las pequeñas moléculas orgánicas son importantes en áreas como la medicina, los sabores y los materiales. La forma en tres dimensiones (3D) de estas moléculas puede cambiar cómo funcionan. Los científicos crean muchas moléculas diferentes uniendo pequeñas partes químicas, pero los tipos de conexiones que podían hacer eran limitados.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Zhang y colegas descubrió una nueva técnica química que permite construir moléculas 3D uniendo átomos de carbono de una manera especial. Estos átomos de carbono ya están unidos a otros tres átomos, formando un tipo de enlace llamado C(sp3)–C(sp3). Este método no solo conecta las partes, sino que también controla con precisión la forma 3D de la molécula.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque ofrece a los científicos una nueva herramienta para crear colecciones de moléculas 3D con formas exactas. Controlar la forma es clave para diseñar mejores medicinas y materiales. Antes, era más difícil hacer estos tipos de conexiones de carbono con control de la forma, por lo que este método abre nuevas posibilidades.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este nuevo método para construir varias moléculas y probarlas en la búsqueda de medicinas y materiales. Esto podría acelerar el proceso de encontrar nuevos medicamentos y materiales innovadores con propiedades útiles.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo método químico permite ahora a los científicos construir moléculas 3D con formas precisas al unir átomos de carbono de forma controlada, ayudando al diseño de medicamentos y materiales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of intricately interlocking three-dimensional molecular structures composed of colorful, geometric building blocks connected by glowing, precise carbon–carbon bonds, arranged like a harmonious, modular puzzle floating in soft natural tones against a gentle, abstract background symbolizing scientific innovation and molecular diversity.

articles.title: Breakthrough computer chip tech could help meet ‘monumental demand’ driven by AI
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01050-5Innovative tool for producing computer chips uses giant, nearly perfect mirrors to make tiny transistors and circuits.
articles.content:
This chip-patterning system uses extreme ultraviolet light to ‘paint’ patterns on silicon wafers.Credit: ASML

A powerful light source bigger than a London double-decker bus has set a record: it can create structures on a silicon wafer that are just 8 nanometres (nm) wide. Those are thought to be the smallest ever made in a single step by a commericial chip-patterning system. According to the system’s manufacturer, it could be used to make computer chips patterned with 2.9 times more transistors than chips produced with the previous generation of the light sources used for this purpose.

The device projects extreme ultraviolet (EUV) light through a patterned ‘mask’ onto the surfaces of silicon wafers coated with light-sensitive chemicals. In response to the light, the chemicals harden in the same pattern. The wafer is then chemically etched, and the process repeated, to produce all of the electric components of the chip, including tiny switches called transistors and the extremely fine wiring that connects them.

How cutting-edge computer chips are speeding up the AI revolution

This process, called EUV lithography, is not new. But the record-setting model has extra-powerful optics that can make smaller transistors. Cramming ever-more and tinier transistors into a chip of a given area enables progress in computing. Chips with more transistors could also help artificial-intelligence data centres to run more computations without using more electricity.

The unprecedented system was described at the SPIE Advanced Lithography + Patterning conference in San Jose, California, in February1. The advances were presented by a representative of the system’s manufacturer, ASML, which is headquartered in Veldhoven, the Netherlands.

The company has sent about ten of these EUV devices, which cost about US$400 million, to its customers, including chipmakers Intel and SK hynix. These companies will use the machines to create their next generation of chips, says Maarten Voncken, head of research metrology at ASML. Thanks to the AI boom, says Voncken, “the demands we see are monumental in the number of chips that are needed and the scaling that is needed”.

Pushing Moore’s law

The high-performance computer chips used in mobile phones and the data centres powering the AI boom have features crafted with nearly atomic-scale precision. A guiding principle of the chip industry called Moore’s Law dictates that the number of transistors on a chip should double roughly every two years. It’s not a law of nature, but it has held true thanks to the tremendous efforts of engineers and physicists. But it’s getting harder to keep up with Moore’s law, even as AI creates a demand for ever-faster chips.

Improvements in lithography systems can help. The shorter the wavelength of light, the smaller the features that can be etched onto a wafer. The properties of the light-handling systems are also important. Systems with higher values of a unitless property called numerical aperture can emit light over a broader range of angles. This results in better image contrast and therefore higher resolution, meaning chipmakers can use these systesm to pack higher densities of transistors onto chips.

From lunatic fringe to data centres

The lithography tools used in the 1990s and into the 2000s used deep ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 193 nm, the shortest wavelength that can be directed using the types of lens found in eyeglasses and binoculars.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in computer chip manufacturing using extreme ultraviolet lithography, enabling smaller transistors and higher transistor density. This advancement supports the AI revolution by allowing more powerful and energy-efficient chips, which has broad societal and economic impact given the increasing demand for AI computing power. The article provides detailed context about the technology, its significance, and its potential benefits, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New chip technology creates tinier transistors to boost AI computing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Computer chips are made by etching tiny patterns on silicon wafers. Smaller patterns mean more transistors, which help chips work faster and use less energy. This is important for powering things like artificial intelligence (AI).

**What happened** – A company called ASML built a huge machine that uses extreme ultraviolet (EUV) light to create the smallest transistor patterns ever made in one step—just 8 nanometres wide. This machine is bigger than a London double-decker bus and can fit nearly three times more transistors on a chip than older machines.

**Impact** – Making chips with more and smaller transistors helps computers, especially AI data centers, run more calculations while using less electricity. This breakthrough keeps improving chips possible, even as it becomes harder to make them smaller. It supports the growing demand for powerful AI technology.

**What's next step** – ASML has already sent about ten of these machines to companies like Intel and SK hynix. These companies will use the new machines to make the next generation of advanced chips. This technology may help AI systems become faster and more efficient in the near future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new chip-making machine uses powerful light to create tinier transistors, enabling more powerful and energy-efficient chips for AI technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle technologie de puce crée des transistors plus petits pour renforcer l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les puces informatiques sont fabriquées en gravant de minuscules motifs sur des plaquettes de silicium. Des motifs plus petits signifient plus de transistors, ce qui rend les puces plus rapides et moins énergivores. Cela est important pour faire fonctionner des technologies comme l’intelligence artificielle (IA).

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise appelée ASML a construit une énorme machine qui utilise la lumière extrême ultraviolette (EUV) pour créer les plus petits motifs de transistors jamais réalisés en une seule étape – seulement 8 nanomètres de large. Cette machine est plus grande qu’un bus londonien à impériale et peut placer près de trois fois plus de transistors sur une puce que les machines précédentes.

**Impact** – Fabriquer des puces avec plus de transistors plus petits aide les ordinateurs, surtout les centres de données d’IA, à effectuer plus de calculs tout en consommant moins d’électricité. Cette avancée permet de continuer à améliorer les puces alors que leur miniaturisation devient plus difficile. Elle répond à la demande croissante de technologies d’IA puissantes.

**Prochaine étape** – ASML a déjà livré une dizaine de ces machines à des entreprises comme Intel et SK hynix. Ces entreprises utiliseront ces nouvelles machines pour créer la prochaine génération de puces avancées. Cette technologie pourrait rendre les systèmes d’IA plus rapides et efficaces bientôt.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle machine de fabrication de puces utilise une lumière puissante pour créer des transistors plus petits, permettant des puces plus puissantes et économes en énergie pour l’IA.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva tecnología de chips crea transistores más pequeños para potenciar la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los chips de computadora se fabrican grabando patrones muy pequeños en obleas de silicio. Patrones más pequeños significan más transistores, lo que hace que los chips funcionen más rápido y usen menos energía. Esto es importante para tecnologías como la inteligencia artificial (IA).

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada ASML construyó una máquina enorme que usa luz ultravioleta extrema (EUV) para crear los patrones de transistores más pequeños hechos en un solo paso: solo 8 nanómetros de ancho. Esta máquina es más grande que un autobús de dos pisos de Londres y puede poner casi tres veces más transistores en un chip que las máquinas anteriores.

**Impacto** – Fabricar chips con más transistores más pequeños ayuda a las computadoras, especialmente a los centros de datos de IA, a hacer más cálculos usando menos electricidad. Este avance mantiene posible la mejora de los chips aunque sea más difícil hacerlos más pequeños. Apoya la creciente demanda de tecnología de IA poderosa.

**Próximo paso** – ASML ya ha enviado unas diez de estas máquinas a empresas como Intel y SK hynix. Estas compañías usarán las nuevas máquinas para fabricar la próxima generación de chips avanzados. Esta tecnología podría ayudar a que los sistemas de IA sean más rápidos y eficientes pronto.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva máquina para fabricar chips usa luz potente para crear transistores más pequeños, permitiendo chips más potentes y eficientes para la tecnología de IA.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vast, glowing silicon wafer patterned with intricate, tiny circuit lines illuminated by a radiant beam of extreme ultraviolet light shaped like a precise, elegant brushstroke, set against a softly lit, abstract background symbolizing advanced technology and innovation.
